title_en: Great Treatise on the Correspondences of Yin and Yang to Phenomena
paragraphs_en: Huangdi said: Yin and yang are the way of heaven and earth, the fundamental principle of the myriad things, the parents of change and transformation, the root and origin of life and death, the abode of spirit and brightness. In treating disease, one must always seek the root. Thus accumulated yang forms heaven, and accumulated yin forms earth. Yin is tranquil and yang is restless; yang generates, and yin matures; yang kills, and yin stores away. Yang transforms into qi, and yin forms into physical shape. When cold reaches its extreme, it generates heat; when heat reaches its extreme, it generates cold. Cold qi generates turbidity; heat qi generates clarity. When clear qi lies below, it causes undigested diarrhea; when turbid qi lies above, it causes distension and bloating. This is what happens when yin and yang act contrary to their proper places — the reversal and compliance that underlie disease.

Thus clear yang forms heaven, and turbid yin forms earth; the qi of earth rises to become clouds, and the qi of heaven descends to become rain. Rain issues from the qi of earth, and clouds issue from the qi of heaven.

Thus clear yang emerges from the upper orifices, and turbid yin emerges from the lower orifices; clear yang issues forth at the interstitial spaces of the skin, and turbid yin moves through the five viscera; clear yang fills and strengthens the four limbs, and turbid yin returns to the six bowels.

Water is yin, and fire is yang. Yang is qi, and yin is flavor. Flavor nourishes physical form, physical form is sustained by qi, qi is generated from essence, and essence arises through transformation. Essence is nourished by qi, and physical form is nourished by flavor; transformation generates essence, and qi generates physical form. Flavor, if excessive, injures physical form; qi, if excessive, injures essence. Essence transforms into qi, and qi may in turn be injured by flavor. Yin flavors exit through the lower orifices, and yang qi exits through the upper orifices. Flavors that are strong belong to yin; those that are mild belong to the yang aspect of yin. Qi that is strong belongs to yang; that which is mild belongs to the yin aspect of yang. Strong flavor causes purging; mild flavor allows free passage. Mild qi causes venting and dispersal; strong qi generates heat. The qi of vigorous fire weakens, while the qi of mild fire flourishes. Vigorous fire consumes qi, and qi is nourished by mild fire. Vigorous fire disperses qi, while mild fire generates qi. As for qi and flavor: the acrid and the sweet, which disperse outward, belong to yang; the sour and the bitter, which induce vomiting and purging, belong to yin.

When yin prevails, yang becomes diseased; when yang prevails, yin becomes diseased. When yang prevails, there is heat; when yin prevails, there is cold. Extreme cold generates heat; extreme heat generates cold. Cold injures physical form; heat injures qi. Injury to qi causes pain; injury to physical form causes swelling. Thus, when pain precedes swelling, it means qi has injured the physical form; when swelling precedes pain, it means the physical form has injured qi. When wind prevails, there is movement and tremor; when heat prevails, there is swelling; when dryness prevails, there is desiccation; when cold prevails, there is puffiness and edema; when dampness prevails, there is diarrhea with loose, watery stool.

Heaven has the four seasons and the five phases, by which it generates, grows, gathers in, and stores away, and by which it produces cold, summer heat, dryness, dampness, and wind. Man has the five viscera, which transform into five qi, giving rise to joy, anger, sorrow, worry, and fear. Thus joy and anger injure qi, while cold and summer heat injure the physical form. Sudden rage injures yin; sudden joy injures yang. When rebellious qi surges upward, it fills the vessels and departs from the physical form. If joy and anger are not moderated, and cold and heat exceed their proper bounds, then life cannot be secured. Thus extreme yin will inevitably turn into yang, and extreme yang will inevitably turn into yin. Hence it is said: if one is injured by cold in winter, one will certainly suffer a warm disease in spring; if injured by wind in spring, one will develop undigested diarrhea in summer; if injured by summer heat in summer, one will certainly suffer intermittent fevers in autumn; if injured by dampness in autumn, one will in time develop cough.

Huangdi said: I have heard that the sages of high antiquity, in discussing and ordering the human body, distinguished and classified the viscera and bowels, traced and connected the channels and vessels, joined and linked the six confluences, each following its own channel; the qi-points that arise each have their own location and name; the valleys and ravines belonging to the bones each have their own point of origin; the divisions and regions, whether contrary or compliant, each have their own order and pattern; the yin and yang of the four seasons all have their governing sequence; and the correspondences between outer and inner all have their exterior and interior aspects. Is this truly so? Qibo replied: The east generates wind; wind generates wood; wood generates the sour flavor; the sour flavor generates the liver; the liver generates the sinews; the sinews generate the heart; the liver governs the eyes. In heaven this is the mysterious and profound; in man it is the Way; on earth it is transformation. Transformation generates the five flavors; the Way generates wisdom; the mysterious and profound generates spirit. Spirit in heaven is wind, on earth is wood, in the body is the sinews, in the viscera is the liver, in color is verdant green, in musical note is jue, in the vocal sound is shouting, in movement is grasping, in the orifice is the eyes, in flavor is sour, in emotion is anger. Anger injures the liver; sorrow overcomes anger. Wind injures the sinews; dryness overcomes wind. Sour flavor injures the sinews; the acrid flavor overcomes the sour. The south generates heat; heat generates fire; fire generates the bitter flavor; the bitter flavor generates the heart; the heart generates blood; blood generates the spleen; the heart governs the tongue. In heaven this is heat, on earth is fire, in the body is the vessels, in the viscera is the heart, in color is red, in musical note is zhi, in the vocal sound is laughter, in movement is anxious agitation, in the orifice is the tongue, in flavor is bitter, in emotion is joy. Joy injures the heart; fear overcomes joy. Heat injures qi; cold overcomes heat. Bitter flavor injures qi; the salty flavor overcomes the bitter. The center generates dampness; dampness generates earth; earth generates the sweet flavor; the sweet flavor generates the spleen; the spleen generates flesh; flesh generates the lung; the spleen governs the mouth. In heaven this is dampness, on earth is earth, in the body is the flesh, in the viscera is the spleen, in color is yellow, in musical note is gong, in the vocal sound is singing, in movement is retching, in the orifice is the mouth, in flavor is sweet, in emotion is pensiveness. Pensiveness injures the spleen; anger overcomes pensiveness. Dampness injures the flesh; wind overcomes dampness. Sweet flavor injures the flesh; the sour flavor overcomes the sweet. The west generates dryness; dryness generates metal; metal generates the acrid flavor; the acrid flavor generates the lung; the lung generates the skin and body hair; the skin and body hair generate the kidney; the lung governs the nose. In heaven this is dryness, on earth is metal, in the body is the skin and body hair, in the viscera is the lung, in color is white, in musical note is shang, in the vocal sound is weeping, in movement is coughing, in the orifice is the nose, in flavor is acrid, in emotion is worry. Worry injures the lung; joy overcomes worry. Heat injures the skin and body hair; cold overcomes heat. Acrid flavor injures the skin and body hair; the bitter flavor overcomes the acrid. The north generates cold; cold generates water; water generates the salty flavor; the salty flavor generates the kidney; the kidney generates the bone marrow; the marrow generates the liver; the kidney governs the ears. In heaven this is cold, on earth is water, in the body is the bones, in the viscera is the kidney, in color is black, in musical note is yu, in the vocal sound is groaning, in movement is shivering, in the orifice is the ears, in flavor is salty, in emotion is fear. Fear injures the kidney; pensiveness overcomes fear. Cold injures the blood; dryness overcomes cold. Salty flavor injures the blood; the sweet flavor overcomes the salty. Hence it is said: heaven and earth are the above and below of the myriad things; yin and yang are the male and female of blood and qi; left and right are the pathways of yin and yang; water and fire are the manifest signs of yin and yang; yin and yang are the capacity and origin of the myriad things. Hence it is said: yin, dwelling within, is that which yang guards and preserves; yang, dwelling without, is that which yin puts to use and directs.

Huangdi said: How does one take yin and yang as the model? Qibo said: When yang prevails, the body is hot, the interstitial spaces of the skin are closed, breathing is coarse and labored, causing one to bend forward and back; sweat does not come out, and heat persists; the teeth become dry, and there is vexation, oppression, and abdominal fullness — this is fatal. Such a person can endure winter but not summer. When yin prevails, the body is cold and sweats, the body is habitually cool, one shivers repeatedly and feels cold; when cold reaches an extreme it becomes reversal, and reversal causes abdominal fullness — this is fatal. Such a person can endure summer but not winter. These are the transformations that occur when yin and yang alternately prevail — the manifest forms and states of disease.

Huangdi said: How does one harmonize these two? Qibo said: If one is able to understand the seven depletions and eight benefits, then the two can be harmonized; if one does not know how to apply this, then the season of early decline sets in. At forty years of age, one's yin qi is already reduced by half, and daily activity begins to decline. At fifty, the body feels heavy, and the ears and eyes are no longer sharp and clear. At sixty, yin withers, qi greatly declines, the nine orifices no longer function smoothly, there is deficiency below and excess above, and tears and mucus flow together. Hence it is said: those who understand this remain strong, and those who do not grow old — though they issue from the same source, they come to differ in name. The wise observe what is common; the foolish observe what is different. The foolish are insufficient; the wise have surplus. When there is surplus, the ears and eyes are sharp and clear, the body is light and strong, the old are restored to vigor, and the vigorous become even more sound. This is why the sage attends to the affairs of non-action, delights in the capacity for calm and contentment, follows his desires and pleases his intent within the guarding of emptiness and nothingness — and thus his years and life are without limit, ending only together with heaven and earth. This is how the sage cultivates the body.

Heaven is insufficient in the northwest, hence the northwest is yin, and in man the right ear and eye are not as sharp as the left. Earth is not full in the southeast, hence the southeast is yang, and in man the left hand and foot are not as strong as the right. Huangdi said: Why is this so? Qibo said: The east is yang; in yang, its essence gathers above — gathering above, the upper part is bright and clear while the lower part is deficient, and so the ears and eyes are sharp and clear, but the hands and feet are less agile. The west is yin; in yin, its essence gathers below — gathering below, the lower part is abundant while the upper part is deficient, and so the ears and eyes are not sharp and clear, but the hands and feet are agile. Thus when both are affected by pathogenic influence, if it is above the right side is more severely affected, if it is below the left side is more severely affected — this is because heaven and earth, and yin and yang, cannot themselves be entirely complete, and so pathogenic qi lodges in the incomplete places. Thus heaven possesses essence, and earth possesses physical form; heaven has its eight guiding sequences, and earth has its five patterns, and so they are able to serve as the parents of the myriad things. Clear yang ascends to heaven, and turbid yin returns to earth; thus the movement and stillness of heaven and earth are governed by spirit and brightness as their guiding principle, and so they are able to generate, grow, gather in, and store, ending only to begin again. Only the worthy person, above, aligns with heaven to nourish the head; below, models earth to nourish the feet; and in the middle, draws on human affairs to nourish the five viscera. The qi of heaven communicates with the lung; the qi of earth communicates with the throat; the qi of wind communicates with the liver; the qi of thunder communicates with the heart; the qi of grain communicates with the spleen; the qi of rain communicates with the kidney. The six channels are like rivers; the intestines and stomach are like the sea; the nine orifices are the qi through which water pours forth. Taking heaven and earth as yin and yang: the sweat of yang is named after the rain of heaven and earth; the qi of yang is named after the swift wind of heaven and earth. Sudden, violent qi resembles thunder; rebellious qi resembles yang. Thus, if in treatment one does not model the guiding sequences of heaven and does not apply the principles of earth, calamity and harm will arrive.

Thus when pathogenic wind arrives, it is as swift as wind and rain. Therefore the skilled physician treats the skin and body hair; the next best treats the muscles and flesh; the next treats the sinews and vessels; the next treats the six bowels; and the next treats the five viscera. One who must treat the five viscera has an even chance of life and death. Thus the pathogenic qi of heaven, if it invades, harms the person's five viscera; the cold and heat of food and drink, if they invade, harm the six bowels; the damp qi of earth, if it invades, harms the skin, flesh, sinews, and vessels. Therefore the physician skilled in the use of needles draws yang from yin and draws yin from yang, treats the left to address the right and treats the right to address the left, uses himself to understand the other, uses the exterior to know the interior, and observes the principle of excess and insufficiency; by perceiving the subtle he grasps the fault, and so his use of the needle is never dangerous. The physician skilled in diagnosis examines the complexion and palpates the pulse, first distinguishing yin and yang; he discerns clarity and turbidity, and thereby knows the region of disease; he observes the breathing and listens to the voice, and thereby knows the source of suffering; he observes the seasonal pulse standards, and thereby knows what governs the disease. He presses the cubit and inch pulse positions, observes whether the pulse is floating, sunken, slippery, or rough, and thereby knows where the disease arises; in treating, he makes no error, and in diagnosing, he loses nothing. Hence it is said: when a disease first arises, it can be resolved by needling alone; when it is at its height, one must wait for it to wane before it can be resolved. Thus, according to its lightness, disperse it upward and outward; according to its severity, diminish it; according to its decline, restore and make it manifest again. When the physical form is insufficient, warm it with qi; when essence is insufficient, supplement it with flavor. When the disease is high in the body, induce vomiting to expel it; when it is low, draw it out and drain it away; when there is fullness in the middle, purge it from within; when there is pathogenic qi present, soak the body to induce sweating; when it is in the skin, sweat it out and disperse it; when it is fierce and swift, press and restrain it; when it is excess, disperse and drain it. Examine the yin and yang to distinguish the soft from the hard: treat yang disease through yin, and yin disease through yang; fix the blood and qi so that each keeps to its own place. When blood is in excess, one should drain it off; when qi is deficient, one should guide and lead it.

title_en: Treatise on the Separation and Union of Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have heard that heaven is yang and earth is yin, the sun is yang and the moon is yin; that the greater and lesser months together make three hundred sixty days forming one year, and that man corresponds to this as well. Now, the three yin and three yang do not seem to correspond simply to yin and yang — what is the reason for this? Qibo replied: Yin and yang can be counted to ten, extended to a hundred, counted to a thousand, extended to ten thousand; and beyond ten thousand they become too great to be counted in full — yet their essential principle is one. Heaven covers and earth supports, and the myriad things are thereby born. That which has not yet emerged from the earth is called dwelling in yin, and is termed yin within yin; that which has emerged from the earth is termed yang within yin. Yang bestows uprightness, and yin serves as its master. Thus birth depends on spring, growth depends on summer, gathering-in depends on autumn, and storage depends on winter; when this normal order is lost, the four directions of heaven and earth become obstructed. The transformations of yin and yang, as they occur in man, can likewise be counted and enumerated.

Huangdi said: I would like to hear about the separation and union of the three yin and three yang. Qibo said: When the sage stands facing south, the front is called Vast Brightness, and the rear is called the Great Thoroughfare. The region of the Great Thoroughfare is called Shaoyin (Lesser Yin); above Shaoyin is called Taiyang (Greater Yang). Taiyang is rooted at the point Zhiyin and knots at the Gate of Life; it is termed yang within yin. Above the midpoint of the body is called Vast Brightness; below Vast Brightness is called Taiyin (Greater Yin); in front of Taiyin is called Yangming (Yang Brightness). Yangming is rooted at the point Lidui, and is termed yang within yin. The exterior aspect of Jueyin (Reverting Yin) is called Shaoyang (Lesser Yang); Shaoyang is rooted at the point Qiaoyin, and is termed the lesser yang within yin. Thus, in the separation and union of the three yang: Taiyang is the opening, Yangming is the closing, and Shaoyang is the pivot. These three channels must not lose their proper relation to one another; when they beat together without floating apart, this is called the unity of yang.

Huangdi said: I would like to hear about the three yin. Qibo said: The exterior is yang, and the interior is yin; thus the interior belongs to yin, and its thoroughfare lies below, called Taiyin (Greater Yin). Taiyin is rooted at the point Yinbai, and is termed yin within yin. Behind Taiyin is called Shaoyin (Lesser Yin); Shaoyin is rooted at the point Yongquan, and is termed the lesser yin within yin. In front of Shaoyin is called Jueyin (Reverting Yin); Jueyin is rooted at the point Dadun — where yin is cut off from yang — and is termed the utmost yin within yin. Thus, in the separation and union of the three yin: Taiyin is the opening, Jueyin is the closing, and Shaoyin is the pivot. These three channels must not lose their proper relation to one another; when they beat together without sinking away, this is called the unity of yin. Yin and yang flow on unceasingly, accumulating and transmitting to complete one full cycle; qi governs the interior, and physical form governs the exterior, and together they bring each other to completion.

title_en: Treatise on the Differentiation of Yin and Yang
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Man has four channels and twelve followers — what does this mean? Qibo replied: The four channels correspond to the four seasons, and the twelve followers correspond to the twelve months; the twelve months correspond to the twelve vessels. The pulse has yin and yang; one who knows yang knows yin, and one who knows yin knows yang. In yang alone there are five types, and five times five makes twenty-five yang manifestations. What is called the yin pulse is the true visceral pulse; when it appears nakedly, it signals defeat, and defeat inevitably means death. What is called the yang pulse is the yang of the stomach cavity, a pulse still carrying stomach qi. By distinguishing yang, one knows the location of the disease; by distinguishing yin, one knows the appointed time of death or life. The three yang are manifest at the head, and the three yin are manifest at the wrist — these are, in fact, one and the same principle. By distinguishing yang, one knows the times to be wary of in disease; by distinguishing yin, one knows the appointed time of death or life. One must carefully master yin and yang, and need not consult with the multitude. As for yin and yang in the pulse: that which recedes is yin, and that which arrives is yang; that which is still is yin, and that which moves is yang; that which is slow is yin, and that which is rapid is yang. Whenever one grasps the true visceral pulse, devoid of stomach qi: if the liver pulse arrives suspended, cut off, and tense, death comes in eighteen days; if the heart pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in nine days; if the lung pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in twelve days; if the kidney pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in seven days; if the spleen pulse arrives suspended and cut off, death comes in four days.

It is said: when disease arises in the two yang, it manifests in the heart and spleen; there may be difficulty with private, hidden matters, and in women, absence of menstruation. If it progresses, it becomes wind-wasting; if it progresses further, it becomes panting-rushing — this is fatal and cannot be treated. It is said: when disease arises in the three yang, it manifests as chills and fever, and below as carbuncles and swellings, as well as wasting, reversal, and calf pain and numbness. If it progresses, it becomes a withering and loss of luster of the skin; if it progresses further, it becomes hernial swelling. It is said: when disease arises in the one yang, there is shortness of breath, frequent coughing, and frequent diarrhea. If it progresses, it becomes a clutching sensation in the heart; if it progresses further, it becomes obstruction. When disease arises in the two yang and one yin together, it chiefly causes fright, alarm, and back pain, frequent belching and yawning — this is called wind reversal. When disease arises in the two yin and one yang together, there is frequent distension, fullness in the heart region, and frequent belching. When disease arises in the three yang and three yin together, it causes hemiplegia, wasting and flaccidity, and inability to raise the four limbs.

When the pulse beats as one yang, it is called hook-like; when it beats as one yin, it is called downy; when the yang pulse beats forcefully and tense, it is called string-like; when the yang pulse arrives and then abruptly cuts off, it is called stone-like; when yin and yang pulses pass into one another smoothly, it is called flowing. When yin contends within and yang is disturbed without, the ethereal-soul sweat is not contained, the four limbs become cold and reversal arises; when this reversal arises it smokes and steams the lung, causing the person to pant and wheeze. That which yin generates, when harmonized with its root, is called harmony. Thus, when hardness meets hardness, the yang qi is shattered and dispersed, and the yin qi thereupon perishes. When it becomes turbid and disordered, the hard and the soft fail to harmonize, and the qi of the channels is thereupon cut off. Those of the category of dying yin die within no more than three days; those of the category of living yang die within no more than four days. As for what is called living yang and dying yin: transmission from the liver to the heart is called living yang; from the heart to the lung is called dying yin; from the lung to the kidney is called double yin; from the kidney to the spleen is called overturning yin, which is fatal and cannot be treated. Binding in the yang causes swelling of the four limbs. Binding in the yin causes bloody stool of one sheng; a second binding, two sheng; a third binding, three sheng. When yin and yang bind and become skewed, with more yin and less yang, this is called stone water, with swelling of the lower abdomen. Binding of the two yang is called wasting-thirst. Binding of the three yang is called diaphragm obstruction. Binding of the three yin is called water. Binding of one yin and one yang together is called throat blockage. When the yin pulse beats and the yang pulse is distinct and separate, this indicates pregnancy. When yin and yang are both deficient, and there is diarrhea with prolapse, it is fatal. When yang is added onto yin, this is called sweating. When yin is deficient and yang beats forcefully, this is called flooding. When the three yin all beat together, death occurs at midnight on the twentieth day. When the two yin both beat together, death occurs in the evening of the thirteenth day. When the one yin beats alone, death occurs on the tenth day. When the three yang all beat together and drum forcefully, death occurs on the third day. When the three yin and three yang all beat together, there is fullness of the heart and abdomen; if the eruption is complete and private, hidden functions cannot be attained, death occurs on the fifth day. When the two yang both beat together, and the disease is a warm disease, it is fatal and cannot be treated — death occurs within no more than ten days.

title_en: Treatise on the Secret Canon of the Numinous Orchid Chamber
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I would like to hear about the mutual offices and relative rank of the twelve viscera — which are noble and which are lowly? Qibo replied: How thorough a question this is! Allow me to explain it fully. The heart is the office of the sovereign ruler; spirit and brightness issue from it. The lung is the office of the minister and mentor; regulation and moderation issue from it. The liver is the office of the general; planning and deliberation issue from it. The gallbladder is the office of impartial judgment; decision and decisiveness issue from it. The pericardium is the office of the minister-messenger; joy and delight issue from it. The spleen and stomach are the offices of the granary and storehouse; the five flavors issue from them. The large intestine is the office of transmission and conduction; transformation and change issue from it. The small intestine is the office of reception and abundance; the transformation of things issues from it. The kidney is the office of vigor and strength; skill and ingenuity issue from it. The triple burner is the office of the drainage channels; the waterways issue from it. The bladder is the office of the regional capital; the body fluids are stored there, and only through the transformation of qi can they be released.

In all, these twelve offices must not lose their proper relation to one another. Thus, when the sovereign is enlightened, those below are at peace; if one nurtures life on this basis, one will live long, free from danger to the end of one's days, and if one governs the empire on this basis, it will greatly flourish. When the sovereign is not enlightened, the twelve offices fall into danger, the pathways of communication become blocked and impassable, and the physical form is thereby gravely injured; if one nurtures life on this basis, calamity follows, and if one governs the empire on this basis, its very foundation is gravely imperiled. Be warned of this, be warned of this!

The utmost Way lies in the subtle and minute, and its transformations are without end — who can know its origin? How constrained and pressing it is! One who seeks to grasp it must be watchful and vigilant — who can know its essentials? Amid such deep and diligent concern, who is truly skilled? The reckoning of the vague and indistinct arises from the minutest hair's breadth; the reckoning of the hair's breadth arises from measurement; multiplied a thousand-fold, ten-thousand-fold, it may be extended ever greater, and by extending and enlarging it, its concrete form comes to be established. Huangdi said: Excellent indeed! I have heard that the Way of refined brightness and the enterprise of the great sages, in proclaiming and illuminating the great Way, is not to be received save through fasting, purification, and the choosing of an auspicious day. Huangdi thereupon chose an auspicious day and favorable omen, and stored this teaching in the Chamber of the Numinous Orchid, to be handed down and preserved.
